content:
There are nights when my toddler believes bedtime is just a suggestion.
She talks.
She asks for stories.
She asks for water.
She wants one more hug, one more story, one more minute with Amma.
I glance at the clock.
12:15 a.m.
Then 1:00.
Then 1:45.
I know I have to wake up in just a few hours, but somehow that thought never feels as important as the little face looking back at me.
Finally, sometime around 2 a.m., her tiny fingers loosen their grip around my hand. Her breathing becomes slow and steady.
She’s asleep.
Most people would think my day ends there.
It doesn’t.
I quietly step out of the room.
The toys scattered across the living room slowly disappear into their basket.
The kitchen counter gets wiped clean, ready for another busy morning.
The last few things are put back in their place.
It’s a quiet ritual that no one notices.
Not because anyone expects me to do it, but because tomorrow’s version of me will silently thank today’s version.
Only then do I lie down.
By the time my head touches the pillow, it’s almost 3 a.m.
Three hours later, the alarm rings.
The house is still asleep.
I head to the kitchen.
Lunch has to be packed.
Breakfast has to be made.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by noor vasquez photo on UnsplashSoon, emails, meetings, deadlines, and responsibilities will replace the silence of the early morning.
Some mornings, I wonder how I’m functioning on so little sleep.
But then I remember something.
This season won’t last forever.
One day, she’ll fall asleep without asking for one more story.
The toys won’t be scattered across the floor.
The tiny hand searching for mine in the middle of the night will eventually stop reaching.
And perhaps I’ll miss these sleepless nights more than I can imagine today.
Parenthood has a funny way of making us cherish the very moments that once left us exhausted.
So, to every parent quietly resetting the house after midnight…
To every mother waking before sunrise after barely sleeping…
To every father rocking a child back to sleep before another long workday…
I see you.
The world may only notice what you accomplish during the day.
But some of your greatest acts of love happen long after everyone else has gone to bed.
And maybe that’s what parenting really is.
Doing countless little things that no one applauds, simply because someone small depends on you.
If you’re reading this after another sleepless night, I hope you know this:
You are doing better than you think.
One day, these long nights will become beautiful memories.
Until then, we’ll keep showing up – one yawn, one cup of coffee, and one bedtime story at a time.
